metadata.course_name: Stanford CS336 Language Modeling from Scratch

discussions.title: Overview and Purpose of the Lecture
discussions.summary:
This lecture dives into the detailed architecture and training of large language models (LLMs). It covers the specifics of transformer design, including modern changes that you might not see in other classes. The aim is to learn from many models trained by others instead of training all models yourself.

Definitions:
- LM architecture: The design and structure of language models, especially transformers.
- Hyperparameters: Settings like model size or layer sizes that affect training.

Key Concepts:
- Transformers are the main architecture behind LLMs.
- Understanding what changes and what stayed the same in transformer designs helps build better models.

Important Notes:
- The lecturer updated assignments to fix minor bugs, so make sure to pull the latest versions.
- The lecture will show why you implement a slightly different transformer variant than the original paper.
- Learning from the experience of many trained models is efficient.
- The lecture uses PowerPoint slides instead of executable code, but PDFs are available online.

discussions.title: Transformer Architecture Recap and Modern Variants
discussions.summary:
The lecture reviews the standard transformer design: position embeddings at the bottom, multi-head attention, layer norms, residual connections, MLP (feed-forward layers), and a final softmax. It explains that the implemented transformer has modern tweaks, such as placing layer norms before blocks (pre-norm), rotary position embeddings, and using a special activation called swigloo in the feed-forward layers.

Definitions:
- Transformer: A deep learning model architecture mainly used for language tasks with attention mechanisms.
- Pre-norm: Applying layer normalization before each transformer block.
- Rotary position embeddings: A way to include token position information by rotating embeddings.
- Swigloo: A gated linear unit based activation function used in some modern transformers.
- Layer norm: A technique that normalizes inputs inside the model layers to stabilize training.

Key Concepts:
- Original transformers placed layer norms after blocks (post-norm), but pre-norm is more stable and common now.
- Modern transformers often replace absolute position embeddings with rotary ones.
- Specialized activations like swigloo help improve model performance.

Important Notes:
- The modern transformer variant you implement differs from the original but is better supported by recent research.
- The lecture mentions many recent transformers tweak details but keep the core ideas similar.
- Pre-norm is widely adopted because it stabilizes training and removes the need for complex learning rate warm-ups.

discussions.title: Evolution of Transformer Architectures and LayerNorm Variants
discussions.summary:
Transformer architectures have evolved with many slight changes, especially in how layer normalization is applied. The original transformer used post-norm (layer norm after residual addition), but now almost all use pre-norm (layer norm before residual addition) for better stability. Some new models add layer norms both before and after blocks (double norm). RMS norm, a simplified version of layer norm, has become popular because it's faster and as effective as classic layer norm.

Definitions:
- Post-norm: Applying layer norm after summing residual connections.
- Pre-norm: Applying layer norm before the residual block.
- Double norm: Applying layer norm both before and after a block.
- RMS norm: A simplified layer normalization that normalizes based on root mean square without subtracting mean.

Key Concepts:
- Residual connections provide identity shortcuts that help gradients flow easily through deep networks.
- Putting layer norm inside residuals breaks this identity connection and hurts gradient flow.
- Pre-norm improves training stability and avoids training spikes seen in post-norm.
- RMS norm is faster and saves memory operations without hurting performance.
- Most modern LLMs avoid bias terms in linear layers because it improves training stability.

Important Notes:
- One model (opt 350M) used post-norm by mistake and had unstable training.
- Stability and gradient behavior are key reasons for architectural changes.
- Even small optimizations like RMS norm help since memory access, not just FLOPS, affects speed.
- Removing bias terms is an empirical trick to stabilize large model training.

discussions.title: Activation Functions and Gated Linear Units (GLUs) in Transformers
discussions.summary:
Activation functions are important for transformer performance. While older models used ReLU or GELU, newer models commonly use gated linear units (GLUs) and their variants like swigloo and geglu. These activations gate the hidden activation by multiplying it element-wise with another linear output. This gating improves model quality and has become standard since 2023.

Definitions:
- ReLU: Rectified Linear Unit, outputs zero or the input value whichever is higher.
- GELU: Gaussian Error Linear Unit, smooth version of ReLU using the Gaussian cdf.
- Gated Linear Unit (GLU): An activation where one linear projection is multiplied element-wise by another projection, providing gating.
- Swigloo and Geglu: Specific popular variants of GLU using swish-based activations.

Key Concepts:
- GLUs multiply two linear transformations element-wise to gate activations, improving model expressiveness.
- The gate output dimension is often reduced by 2/3 to keep the total number of parameters similar.
- GLUs slightly increase computational cost but yield better training and final losses.
- Despite improvements, some strong models like GPT-3 still use ReLU or GELU.

Important Notes:
- Non-monotonic behavior in swish-based activations doesn't cause optimization problems due to stochastic gradient descent noise.
- Performance improvements of GLUs are statistically significant in benchmarks.
- GLUs have become widespread because they consistently provide better results while keeping parameter budgets similar.

discussions.title: Sequential vs Parallel Transformer Layers and Architecture Variants
discussions.summary:
Transformer blocks usually process attention and feed-forward operations sequentially (first attention, then MLP), which limits parallelism. Some recent models perform these operations in parallel and combine them before adding to the residual connection. This parallel approach can improve system efficiency and GPU utilization but is less common than the serial design.

Definitions:
- Serial transformer blocks: Processing attention followed by feed-forward layers one after another.
- Parallel transformer blocks: Computing attention and feed-forward layers simultaneously, then summing results.

Key Concepts:
- Serial layers impose ordering, making parallelism across GPUs harder.
- Parallel layers can fuse computations to improve runtime efficiency.
- Most models still use serial layers due to architectural traditions and expressiveness.
- Exceptions using parallel layers include models like Coher Command A, Command R Plus, and Falcon Q 11B.

Important Notes:
- Parallel layers typically allow higher GPU utilization and faster training but might reduce expressiveness slightly.
- Efficiency benefits come from fusing layer norms and linear layers.
- The choice between serial and parallel depends on an engineering trade-off between speed and model capabilities.

discussions.title: Position Embeddings in Transformers: Absolute, Relative, and Rotary
discussions.summary:
Position embeddings add information about token positions in sequences. Early transformers used absolute position embeddings (fixed vectors or learned vectors). Later, relative position embeddings and rotary position embeddings (RoPE) emerged. RoPE encodes positions by rotating embedding vectors, preserving relative distances between tokens.

Definitions:
- Absolute position embeddings: Vectors added to token embeddings to represent absolute positions.
- Relative position embeddings: Represent positions based on distance between tokens.
- RoPE (Rotary Position Embeddings): Encodes relative positions by rotating parts of embeddings, making inner products depend on relative distances.

Key Concepts:
- RoPE splits embeddings into 2D pairs and rotates each by an angle related to token position.
- Rotations preserve inner products, so similarity depends only on relative position.
- RoPE is applied at the attention query/key stage, not at the bottom embedding layer.
- RoPE better supports position invariance and allows models to generalize better to longer contexts.

Important Notes:
- Most modern large language models have converged to using RoPE since 2023.
- Rotation speeds (angles) in RoPE are fixed schedules, not learned parameters.
- RoPE differs from sine/cosine embeddings by preserving relative positioning more exactly and has fewer absolute position leaks.

discussions.title: Hyperparameters: Feed-Forward Dimensions, Aspect Ratios, and Vocabulary Size
discussions.summary:
Choosing hyperparameters like feed-forward size, model width-to-depth ratio, and vocabulary size is important for training effective language models. Commonly, the feed-forward layer size (DFF) is about 4 times the model's hidden dimension (D_model). Gated activations reduce this ratio to about 8/3 to keep parameter counts similar. Most models keep a 1:1 ratio for total hidden dimension to the sum of heads' dimensions. Vocabulary sizes have grown from 30-50k tokens in early models to 100-250k in modern multilingual or production models.

Definitions:
- D_model: The hidden size of the transformer model.
- DFF: Feed-forward hidden dimension inside MLPs; often larger than D_model.
- Aspect ratio: The ratio between model width (hidden size) and depth (number of layers).
- Vocabulary size: Number of unique tokens the model recognizes.

Key Concepts:
- The typical DFF to D_model ratio is 4 for non-gated activations, around 2.6 for GLUs.
- T5 is an example that breaks the convention with a very large feed-forward dimension (65k) compared to 1024 hidden size.
- Models balance width (hidden size) and depth (number of layers) to optimize training performance and hardware utilization.
- Larger vocabularies help multilingual coverage and include emojis or rare symbols.
- Bigger models tend to benefit from larger vocabularies.

Important Notes:
- There is a wide, forgiving range for DFF ratios, so exact tuning is not critical.
- Larger vocabularies reduce inference costs for low-resource or minority languages by better token packing.
- Hyperparameter choices are mostly empirical and conservative, based on what worked in previous models.

discussions.title: Regularization and Weight Decay in Large Language Model Training
discussions.summary:
Although large models train on massive data and typically do not overfit, regularization techniques like weight decay are still used. However, weight decay is used not to prevent overfitting but to improve training stability and final loss by affecting optimization dynamics, especially during learning rate decay at the end of training. Dropout was commonly used in the past but is less popular now.

Definitions:
- Weight decay: A regularization technique that penalizes large weights to prevent overfitting.
- Dropout: Randomly turning off some neurons during training to prevent overfitting.

Key Concepts:
- Weight decay improves training loss and helps optimization in the late phase of training rather than reducing overfitting.
- Learning rate schedules interact with weight decay to accelerate optimization during the late training phase.
- Dropout has gone out of favor because its benefits in large-scale pre-training models are unclear.

Important Notes:
- Large language models often train for only one epoch, making overfitting less likely, which is why dropout and other classic regularizers may be less necessary.
- Weight decay is still common and beneficial, but its effects are linked to training dynamics rather than traditional regularization.
- Not all training details are publicly shared by companies, so some choices are empirical.

discussions.title: Key Stability Techniques in Transformer Training
discussions.summary:
Training large transformers is challenging due to instability like exploding gradients, often caused by softmax operations. Two main techniques improve stability: the Z-loss, which encourages the softmax normalization constant to be near 1, and QK norm, a layer normalization applied to queries and keys before softmax. These reduce numerical problems and allow higher learning rates.

Definitions:
- Z-loss: A regularization loss that forces the softmax normalization constant to stay close to one for numerical stability.
- QK norm: Applying layer normalization to query and key vectors before computing attention scores to stabilize softmax.
- Softmax: A function that turns scores into probabilities, can be numerically unstable due to exponentials and division.

Key Concepts:
- Softmax operations, both at the output layer and inside attention, can cause numerical instability if not handled.
- Z-loss adds a penalty to keep softmax normalization close to ideal.
- QK norm bounds the inputs to softmax, preventing extreme values and improving training.
- Soft capping logits with a tanh function is another technique but hasn't been as effective.

Important Notes:
- These stabilization methods are especially important for large models and longer training.
- Layer normalization has proven remarkably effective in improving stability across many parts of the model.
- Removing these normalization steps at inference would disrupt the model's expected input distributions.

discussions.title: Attention Head Variants and Inference Efficiency Techniques
discussions.summary:
This section discusses new transformer attention variants focusing on inference efficiency: Multi-Query Attention (MQA), Group Query Attention (GQA), and sparse/structured attention patterns for longer contexts. When generating text token-by-token, caching keys and values (KV cache) is used, but memory movement limits throughput. MQA reduces memory by sharing keys and values across heads while keeping multiple query heads. GQA balances between MQA and full multi-head attention. Longer context lengths are supported by techniques like sparse and sliding window attention, and recent architectures combine full attention with local attention for efficiency and extrapolation.

Definitions:
- KV cache: Stores past key and value vectors to speed up autoregressive inference.
- Multi-Query Attention (MQA): Using multiple query heads but a single shared key and value head to reduce memory usage.
- Group Query Attention (GQA): Reduces the number of key/value heads by grouping them, trading off between memory and expressiveness.
- Sparse attention: Attention restricted to subsets of tokens (e.g., local windows), reducing computational cost.
- Sliding window attention: Attention focused only around a token's local neighborhood.

Key Concepts:
- Attention computation is expensive (quadratic in sequence length); inference requires special optimizations.
- MQA improves inference throughput by reducing key/value memory movements without much loss in expressiveness.
- GQA offers a middle ground between full multi-head and MQA by partially sharing keys/values.
- Sparse and sliding window attentions allow transformers to focus on local context, enabling longer sequences.
- Recent models combine a block of full attention without position info with several blocks of sliding window attention with RoPE to balance efficiency and long-range performance.

Important Notes:
- Arithmetic intensity (compute/memory ratio) is key for efficient GPU usage.
- Autoregressive generation is inherently sequential, limiting parallelism.
- These attention variants are important for deploying models at large scale and with long contexts.
- The described techniques emerged from both open research and industrial model releases like GPT-3 and LLaMA.
